text:
In this cross-disciplinary study, Holmqvist presents a new and provocative interpretation of the history of Western strategic thought and how 21st century warfare has come to be presented as a global policing business. Looking at the US-led wars in Afghanistan and Iraq, Holmqvist argues that the dominant liberal perspective on war has come to understand war as a managerial activity aimed at re-establishing a social contract, and thereby creating order in far-off foreign lands.

Expanding Ethical Interstate Violence is the first sociological analysis to unpack the conditions and philosophical underpinnings that made the “policing war on terror” an acceptable ideal for civic elites during the decades before 9/11 was attacked. Erving Holmqvist, combining social and philosophical theory with empirical material, analyses the knowledge frameworks—conceptions of time, space, and human action—that allowed the idea of “the fantasy of policing” to legitimise elite visions and actions even after “the war on terror” had begun to be violently blown off course on the streets of many cities, creating unprecedented internal unrest. Holmqvist traces this evolutionary process in detail, from the years leading up to 9/11 until its full effects played out in reality, leaving liberal conceptions of peaceful international politics open to a sociologically sophisticated reinterpretation of how, in contemporary times, political killings are made compatible with the ideals of the liberalism as a governance system and what significance this holds for researchers within the fields of international relations, war studies and war sociology.
headline: Fully Human Written
